text:
# Abstract: Assembly-Line Justice: A Review of Operation Streamline

Operation Streamline: Summary of Key Findings The report, Operation Streamline: Sanctioning a Broken System, was published by the Warren Institute at UC Berkeley School of Law. Operation Streamline is a zero-tolerance enforcement program that refers all unauthorized border crossers to federal criminal court for prosecution. First-time entrants—even those with no prior record—who are caught crossing the border illegally are prosecuted for the misdemeanor crime of crossing the border unlawfully. The program began in 2005 and has led to a sea change in the way that the border is enforced: instead of being a civil matter that is handled by immigration enforcement officers, border enforcement is now a matter of high-volume criminal processing through the courts. This has produced an enormous number of cases in federal district court. Magistrate judges there are now required to hold on average about 60 hearings per month over many months to deal with cases of defendants who have been apprehended crossing the border illegally, with the hearings sometimes being held en masse with up to 80 or more defendants. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals has recently ruled that such proceedings violate federal law.

Operation Streamline is a program created by the Department of Homeland Security in 2009 to quickly prosecute undocumented immigrants. By using data from courts in Texas, New Mexico and Arizona the study's authors found that the effects of the program on immigration deterrence are doubtful, because the reduction in apprehensions is attributed more to the decline in the economy than to the penalizing effect of prosecution. The enforcement of criminal penalties takes law enforcement away from the real work of law enforcement concerning the narco-traffickers and the traffickers of humans. The federal courts, the U.S. Marshals Service and the American taxpayers are all overburdened by the Streamline program, which threatens the very core of due process that underpins the American system of justice. In short, the "Streamline" program is subversive to the U.S. legal system. The authors call for its abolition in favor of prosecutorial discretion along the lines of the restorative justice model of prosecution advocated by the National Center for Restorative Justice. They also call for the restoration of the immigration system of due process by eliminating penalties for those immigrants who are detained at the border for minor infractions and channeling them into the civil immigration process.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
